items.question: What is the core claim of the universal approximation theorem?
options: Any trained MLP always generalizes perfectly to test data
A wide enough single-hidden-layer MLP with non-linear activation can approximate continuous functions on compact domains
Deep networks are required; shallow networks cannot approximate
Linear models with enough features are universal approximators
items.answer: A wide enough single-hidden-layer MLP with non-linear activation can approximate continuous functions on compact domains
items.explanation: Classic UAT is an existence result for one hidden layer with sufficient width and non-linearity — not a guarantee about training or test performance.

items.question: Which is NOT guaranteed by the universal approximation theorem?
options: Existence of weights that approximate a continuous f* on a bounded region
Generalization to unseen labeled examples
Use of non-linear hidden activations for expressivity
Ability of the MLP hypothesis class to represent non-linear maps
items.explanation: UAT addresses representation on a compact domain. Generalization is a separate statistical property evaluated on held-out data.

items.question: Why must hidden layers use non-linear activations for UAT intuition to apply?
options: Non-linearity speeds up GPU matrix multiply
Without non-linearity, stacked layers collapse to one linear map
Linear activations violate backpropagation
Only non-linear nets can use batch normalization
items.answer: Without non-linearity, stacked layers collapse to one linear map
items.explanation: Linear compositions remain linear — they cannot approximate non-linear continuous targets such as XOR or sin(x).

items.question: Classic UAT primarily concerns network ___ ; depth separation results primarily concern ___.
options: depth; width
width of one hidden layer; parameter efficiency of deeper stacks
generalization; memorization
convolution; recurrence
items.answer: width of one hidden layer; parameter efficiency of deeper stacks
items.explanation: Cybenko/Hornik-style UAT: one wide hidden layer suffices. Depth papers show some functions need many shallow units but fewer deep ones.

items.question: A student trains a huge MLP until training loss ≈ 0 but validation loss is high. The best interpretation is:
options: UAT failed — MLPs cannot represent this problem
The network lacks capacity — add more layers
Expressivity allowed memorization; generalization failed — regularize or add data
Remove hidden layers to fix overfitting
items.answer: Expressivity allowed memorization; generalization failed — regularize or add data
items.explanation: High capacity (UAT-compatible) plus limited/noisy data often yields overfitting — low train error, high val error.

items.question: On which input regions is classic universal approximation typically stated?
options: All of R^n without bounds
Compact (closed and bounded) subsets such as scaled [0,1]^d
Only binary {0,1} inputs
Only integer-valued features
items.answer: Compact (closed and bounded) subsets such as scaled [0,1]^d
items.explanation: Standard UAT proofs target continuous functions on compact domains — motivating feature scaling and bounded operating regions in practice.
